title: Coiled rush bread basket
subtitle: A round flat-based bread basket worked in the coiled rush tradition
excerpt: A coiled rush bread basket: 25 cm diameter, 8 cm sides, a flat base worked in concentric coils of bound rush. Coiling is an older and simpler construction method than willow stake-and-strand; the only tools required are a bodkin and a knife. This project teaches the basic coiling stitch and the technique for building up the sides from the base.
sourceNotes: Traditional British and European coiled rush basketwork; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II for coiled rush preparation and stitching method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A basketry construction method where a continuous bundle or cord is coiled in a spiral and each turn is stitched to the previous one with a binding element.
Dried common bulrush (Scirpus lacustris) or soft rush (Juncus effusus), soaked before use to restore flexibility. The traditional British material for coiled and plaited basketwork.
The thinner strand or strip used to stitch the coils of a coiled basket together. In rush work, a split rush serves as both core and binding.

content.text: Coiled
rush

basketry is the oldest form of basket-making recorded in Britain; neolithic examples survive in waterlogged conditions; and remains the simplest construction method available to a beginner. There is no slath to set up, no stakes to insert, no border technique to learn before starting: the basket grows from a central knot outward in a single continuous spiral. The
coiling
method produces a dense, heavy-based basket that sits firmly on a flat surface.
The rush

Use dried common bulrush or soft rush, available from basketry suppliers in bundles. Soak the rush in cold water for 10 minutes, then wrap in a damp cloth and leave for 30 minutes; the rush should flex without snapping but not be dripping wet. Over-soaked rush goes limp and loses structure; under-soaked rush snaps when bent sharply. Harvest and dry wild rush in late summer if sourcing it directly; it should be pale gold, not green.
Tools
A bodkin for piercing through the coils to pass the
binding element
. A sloyd knife for trimming the rush ends. A leather strop. No other tools are required for coiled rush work.
Method

Start the base: take 6 rush strands and tie them together at the centre with a length of split rush (the binding strand). Coil the 6-strand bundle tightly around the central knot and stitch the binding strand through the centre of the coil with every rotation, using the bodkin to pierce through. Work 3 tight rounds to establish the centre.

Continue the base spiral: add additional rush strands to the bundle as the coil diameter grows; the core bundle should stay at 8 to 10 mm in diameter. Stitch the binding strand over the core and through the previous coil with every stitch, spacing stitches 2 cm apart. Keep the base flat on the work surface as you coil; any tendency to dome upward indicates the stitches are too tight.

Build up the sides: when the base reaches 25 cm diameter, begin angling the core bundle upward rather than outward. The angle of the sides is controlled entirely by the direction you push the new coil; outward for a flat base continuation, upward for the sides. The transition from base to sides should be gradual: 3 or 4 coils at an intermediate angle before going fully vertical.

Continue the sides for 8 cm of height. The sides should be slightly angled outward (not quite vertical) so the basket is slightly wider at the rim than at the base join; this gives visual stability and makes removing a bread loaf easier.

Finish the rim: taper the core bundle to a thin point over the last 8 cm by removing rush strands one by one. Stitch the tapering end tightly to the previous coil, ending with the binding strand wrapped 3 times around the final point and tucked through the coil beneath. Trim the end close with the sloyd knife.
Finishing

Leave the basket to dry in a warm place for 2 days. Rush dries to a pale golden colour and the coils tighten slightly. A lightly damp cloth wiped over the outside of the basket after drying gives a slight sheen without darkening the colour significantly.
Variations
Fruit bowl: same construction, increase the base diameter to 35 cm and omit the sides; end the spiral after the base is complete with a tapered finish.
Coloured stripe: introduce a strand of dyed rush alongside the natural rush in the core bundle for one or two coils to create a stripe band. Rush can be dyed with natural plant dyes before drying.
Care
Do not wash. Wipe clean with a dry or barely damp cloth. Dry rush will mould if kept consistently damp. Store dry between uses.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the coiled rush bread basket.

items.symptom: Base domes upward as the coils progress
items.cause: Binding stitches too tight, pulling each coil inward over the previous one
items.fix: Ease the binding tension on the next 3 rounds and press the base flat on the table after each round. Stitches should lie snugly but not compress the core.

items.symptom: Rush snaps when bending into the coil
items.cause: Insufficient soaking time or rush left too long after soaking
items.fix: Work rush within 30 to 60 minutes of removing it from the damp cloth. Re-dampen any rush that stiffens before use.

items.symptom: Core bundle shrinks too thin as the coil grows
items.cause: Not adding new rush strands to the core as it grows
items.fix: Add one new rush strand to the bundle every 5 cm of coil diameter growth to maintain an 8 to 10 mm core diameter.
